message.content: Why is the sky blue? Explain.

content.text:
The sky appears blue due to a phenomenon called Rayleigh scattering. 

When sunlight enters Earth's atmosphere, it collides with tiny gas molecules (mainly nitrogen and oxygen). These molecules are much smaller than the wavelength of visible light, which causes them to scatter the light in all directions.

Blue light has a shorter wavelength (around 450-495 nanometers) compared to other colors like red or yellow. Shorter wavelengths are scattered much more strongly than longer wavelengths - about 4-5 times more than red light.

As sunlight passes through the atmosphere, the blue light gets scattered in many directions by these molecules, filling the sky with blue light that reaches our eyes from all angles. The other colors pass through more directly with less scattering.

This is why the sky looks blue during the day, and also why sunsets appear red/orange - when the sun is low on the horizon, light travels through more atmosphere, scattering away most of the blue light and leaving the longer red wavelengths to reach us directly.
